ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spicy chicken gizzard rice noodle. Mapped to broader noodle dish category FOODON_00002473 as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice noodles, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for spicy chicken gizzard rice noodle as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite noodle dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional Asian noodle preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of spicy chicken gizzard rice noodle is approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as consumed in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese noodle stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Chicken Gizzard Rice Noodle 泡椒鸡杂小锅米线
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Spicy Chicken Gizzard Rice Noodle (泡椒鸡杂小锅米线), cr_id G00010511, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy chicken gizzard rice noodle as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from rice noodles (boiled) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (rice noodles, boiled: GI ~61, medium category). The protein, fat, fibre, and pickled condiment components of this dish are expected to moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain rice noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 × 4.0) / 100 ≈ 61.5 for a 400 g serving (carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 × 4 = 100.76 g; GL = 61 × 100.76 / 100 = 61.5).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100 g (K:Na ratio >1.4).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced pickled chili (pao jiao) and reduced soy sauce or broth to lower sodium intake, and should limit portion size to no more than 300 g per meal to keep sodium contribution below 700 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with medium GI (~61); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g partially attenuates postprandial glucose rise; sugar is negligible at 0.21 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 250–300 g per serving, pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load, and verify that pickled condiments do not contain added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21 g/100 g is moderate; cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source (see notes); sodium at 231 mg/100 g and pickled condiments contribute to cardiovascular sodium load; iron 2.55 mg/100 g and potassium 327 mg/100 g are cardioprotective micronutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced broth, soy sauce, and pickled chili to minimise sodium intake, keep portion size to 300 g or less, and note that chicken gizzard is an organ meat that may contain higher saturated fat than the HPB composite figure reflects — detailed fatty acid data are not available in the source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pickled chili (pao jiao) is a nightshade ingredient; solanine-related joint sensitivity is a recognised concern in RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request chili-free preparation to eliminate nightshade exposure, limit portion size due to organ-meat cholesterol content, and consider adding anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or turmeric.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Chicken Gizzard Rice Noodle (泡椒鸡杂小锅米线, Pào Jiāo Jī Zá Xiǎo Guō Mǐ Xiàn) is a Chinese-origin noodle dish popular in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese noodle stalls, with culinary roots in Yunnan and Sichuan provinces of China. The dish consists of smooth, slippery rice noodles served in a savoury, mildly spicy broth, topped with tender slices of chicken gizzard (jī zá), pickled green chili peppers (pao jiao), garlic, ginger, spring onion, and chili oil. The rice noodles provide a soft, chewy texture, while the chicken gizzard contributes a firm, slightly chewy bite and a rich umami flavour. The pickled chili (pao jiao) imparts a tangy, fermented heat that distinguishes this dish from other rice noodle preparations. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The moderate glycaemic index (~61) and meaningful fibre content make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content from broth, soy sauce, and pickled condiments is a key consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy as an allergen. It is a common lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese food culture.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010511) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact — the HPB entry corresponds to this specific dish by name and cr_id.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for G00010511 as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g in the HPB source. This is noted as potentially anomalous given that chicken gizzard is an organ meat typically containing measurable cholesterol; however, the HPB value is accepted as reported from the approved source. The value 0.0 is confirmed per HPB source and is not a zero-macro justification issue as cholesterol is not a core macro. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for rice noodles (boiled); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pickled chili and soy sauce whose halal certification cannot be confirmed without vendor verification; the base ingredients (chicken, rice noodles) are potentially halal but the composite dish halal status is unverified. 5. FoodEx2 code A0FJP and FoodOn FOODON_00002473 are broader-category anchors; no specific ontology code exists for this composite dish.
